title: FINS3616 Week 5 Updated
body:
FINS3616 INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS FINANCE

LECTURE 5 EXCHANGE RATE TRADING AND FORECASTING

Janghoon Shon (University of New South Wales)  ILAB ASSIGNMENT

Format: Moodle submission Due: Friday, July 18th, 17:00 % Evaluation: 15% The task of the project is to forecast foreign exchange rates using regression models and to understand how to use forecast information. Each student is required to attend an iLab session in Week 3 or Week 4 in order to learn how to use the FACTSET Database to access historical exchange rate data and macroeconomic information. Students are required to download historical data from the FACTSET database and answer the list of assigned questions. Each student will submit an individual assignment online through Moodle in Week 7. The specific details of the project, as well as the specific assessment criteria, will be available on Moodle by Week 4. Students are also expected to conduct their own research for this assignment.

 2  PLAN

W1: Introduction to International finance & exchange rates W2: Description of FX markets & analysis of exchange rate risk W3: International parity conditions W4: Exchange rate systems and fundamentals W5: Exchange rate trading and forecasting W6: Flexi Week W7: Foreign currency derivatives W8: FX risk hedging and management within multinationals W9: International corporate issues W10: International financing: cross-listing and Eurobonds

 3  TODAY

1) Exchange rate speculation with forwards • Investment profitability without exchange rate hedging • Speculation on the direction of the exchange rate

2) Forward premium and expected exchange rate • Relationship between forward premium and expected exchange rate

3) Exchange rate forecasting models • Fundamentals • Technical analysis and momentum

 4  FORECASTING EXCHANGE RATE

Exchange Rate as a Random Walk (Meese & Rogoff, 1983) “This study compares the out-of-sample forecasting accuracy of various structural and time series exchange rate models. We find that a random walk model performs as well as any estimated model at one-to-twelve-month horizons for the dollar/pound, dollar/mark, dollar/yen and trade-weighted dollar exchange rates".

40 years later…. It is still a prevailing view that exchange rate changes are very difficult to predict in the short term.

 5  FORECASTING EXCHANGE RATE

Bekaert & Hodrick (2013) “Is it reasonable to expect exchange rate forecasts to be characterized with large variability? We think the answer is yes because exchange rates are the relative prices of currencies and currencies are assets. Thus, exchange rates are asset prices, such as stock prices, and we should expect exchange rates to behave very much like other asset prices, such as stock prices, which are also very difficult to predict. If exchange rates were easy to predict, lots of easy money would be made betting that one currency would strengthen relative to another."

 6  FORECASTING EXCHANGE RATE

You have an uncle in the import-export business in New York. • He often ships goods from Australia. • His invoices are due at the end of each month.

He is wondering whether, depending on the respective interest rates, it is better to ❖ hold his money in the US for a month and convert later (on the payment day) ❖ or to convert immediately into AUD and hold the money in Australia.

Have this in the back of your minds, for the next few slides.

 7   / Introduction to international finance /

Investment profitability without currency hedging

Excess return of 1 AUD invested in USD (Australian investor):

Rt+1 = (1+rUSD)St+1/St - (1+rAUD) with S = AUD/USD and r = interest rate

ΔSt+1 > 0 => Appreciation of the USD = increase in return Rt+1

 9

Investment profitability without currency hedging

Exchange rate level at which Rt+1 = 0:

S*t+1 = St(1+rAUD)/(1+rUSD)

Reminder: covered interest rate parity (if S and F=AUD/USD, see lecture 1)

Ft,t+1 = St(1+rAUD)/(1+rUSD)

The forward exchange rate is thus the natural reference point for calculating profits or losses of a currency position.

•Profit if St+1 > S*t+1 = Ft,t+1

•Loss if St+1 < S*t+1 = Ft,t+1

 10

Forward contract (reminder)

A forward contract is an agreement between two parties to buy or sell a security (or currency) at a pre-specified price on a pre-specified date T.

The party agreeing to buy (sell) the underlying currency in the future is said to “buy (sell) a forward” and will have a long (short) position in that currency.

• Mr. Buy buys USD forward with AUD from Ms. Sell

• Mr. Buy’s payoff = St+1 – Ft,t+1

• Ms. Sell sells USD forward for AUD from Mr. Buy

• Ms. Sell’s payoff = Ft,t+1 – St+1

 11

Profits and losses from a forward position

Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2013)  12

Investment profitability without currency hedging

Excess return of 1 AUD invested in USD (Australian investor):

Rt+1 = (1+rUSD)St+1/St - (1+rAUD)

with S = AUD/USD and r = interest rate

ΔSt+1 > 0 => Appreciation of the USD = increase in return Rt+1

 13

Speculation strategy on the direction of the exchange rate

The forward contract therefore allows you to speculate on the direction of the exchange rate.

• Need to compare the expected exchange rate with the forward exchange rate:

• Long position if Et(St+1) > Ft,t+1 (long USD)

• Short position if Et(St+1) < Ft,t+1 (short USD)

Return calculation on a forward position:

RF,t+1 = (St+1 - Ft,t+1 )/St

 14

Example of forecasts Value ex post Change ex post (%) Spot (Yen/$) 96.52 100.65 10.7 15.5 23.05.1995: 87.18 Forecast Yen/$ Forecast error (%) 3 month 6 month 3 month 6 month Futures 86.11 85.08 11.9 17.9

S. G. Warburg 88 103 9.8 -2.7 Union Bank of Switz. 92 96 5.2 5.3 Goldman Sachs 85 95 13.2 6.5 Citibank 82 75 16.7 29.4 Paine Webber Intl. 92 90 5.2 12.2 Merrill Lynch 88 92 9.8 9.9 Swiss Bank Corp. 93 90 4 12.2

Data source: Wall Street Journal, May 25, 1995, p. C1.  15

Example of forecasts

• Strong divergence of forecasts

• Forecasts different from the rate of forward contracts

• Large forecast errors, ex post

Every bank hopes to make a profit

• S. G. Warburg: buys USD forward in 6 months.

Expected (subjective) profit of (103 - 85.08)/87.18 = 20.6%.

• Citibank: sells USD forwards in 6 months

Expected (subjective) profit of (85.08 - 75)/87.18 = 11.6%.

Both were wrong, but Citi even got the direction wrong!  16

Survey forecasts: disagreement across professionals

Data source: https://www.consensuseconomics.com  17

Are professional forecasts useful?

“We find some ability in the survey data to predict future movements in the exchange rate (and in the right direction!).”

Chinn and Frankel (2002)

How do forecasters do it?

Frankel and Froot (1990): at short horizons they extrapolate recent trends (technical analysis) while at longer horizons they tend to forecast a return to a long-term equilibrium, such as purchasing power parity.

More details later in the lecture.

 18

And more recently, do these professional forecasts work?

“Survey expectations successfully forecast exchange rate movements over a two-year horizon both in and out of sample.”

“By contrast, they are considerably less successful in predicting exchange rate appreciation over shorter horizons.”

Kremens, Martin, and Varela (2024)

Which information do the forecasts capture?

• Currency risk premium (covariance of the exchange rate with the S&P 500 index). Think of a CAPM story.

• The real exchange rate (or PPP, as in Dahlquist and Penasse (2022))

• The ratio of current account balance to GDP (see last week)

 19   / Introduction to international finance /

EXPECTED EXCHANGE RATES AND THE FORWARD PREMIUM

Forward premium

• USD is quoted "at a forward premium" if Ft,T > St (AUD/USD)

• USD is quoted "at a forward discount" if Ft,T < St (AUD/USD)

Intuition:

You need to pay more AUD for one USD in the future if premium.

The forward premium is typically in % and annualized:

(12/N) *(Ft,t+N/12 – St )/St

with N = number of months of the contract

 21

Forward premium example

Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2013)

 22

Recall the

Covered interest parity (CIP): Ft,t+1 = St(1+rAUD)/(1+rUSD)

Uncovered interest parity (UIP): Et(St+1) = St (1+rAUD)/(1+rUSD)

with S and F expressed as AUD/USD (AUD for 1 USD)

Let’s combine UIP with CIP:

Et(St+1) = Ft,t+1

UIP (together with CIP) implies that forward exchange rate is an unbiased predictor of the future spot exchange rate.

Forecast error = 0 on average (definition of unbiased).

 23

Making the theory testable

The unbiasedness hypothesis states that Et(St+1) = Ft,t+1

We can rewrite this as: Et St+1 −St = Ft,t+1 −St Et St+1 −St Ft,t+1 −St = St St

Let’s now denote the expected currency return by Et St+1 −St Ft,t+1 −St Et st+1 ≡ and the forward premium by ft,t+1 ≡ . St St

We obtain E(st+1 ) = ft,t+1 ≈ rtAUD − rtUSD , with S=AUD/USD.

 24

Rational expectations

st+1 = E(st+1) + εt+1

Realized appreciation = Expected appreciation/depreciation + Forecasting error

Unbiasedness hypothesis

E(st+1) = ft,t+1

Expected appreciation/depreciation = Forwards premium/discount

By combining the two equations,

st+1 = ft,t+1 + εt+1

Realized appreciation/depreciation = Forwards premium/discount + Forecasting error

 25

Empirical test

We can thus test the validity of

st+1 = ft,t+1 + εt+1

by estimating the following regression:

st+1 = a + b ft,t+1+ εt+1

What should the coefficients a and b be for UIP to hold?

 26

Empirical test

st+1 = a + b ft,t+1+ εt+1

Results:

• Constant a is significantly different from 0

• Coefficient b<0 and significantly different from 1

Baillie and Bollerslev (2000), Bekaert and Hodrick (2012)

Implications

The constant a is in the right direction (higher interest rate currencies depreciate, on average, in the long run)

The slope b suggests that UIP does not hold in short-term: countries with higher interest rates = future currency appreciation!

Possible explanation: risk premium (see in a few slides)  27

Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2013)  28

Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2013)

Interpretation:

a = -10.03: USD at a discount because rjpy < rusd , we have that USD depreciates on average.

b = -2.18: If USD becomes more at a discount because rjpy – rusd is more < 0, we should have USD depreciating but we find that it appreciates!

 29

Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2013)  30

What can explain the UIP failure? A risk premium story

Assume that the true model of exchange rate changes is as follows:

E(𝑠𝑡+1 ) = 𝑓𝑡,𝑡+1 + 𝑅𝑃𝑡 ,

where 𝑅𝑃𝑡 is a risk premium adjustment.

• If the risk premium is always 0, UIP holds.

• But we have seen it fails empirically.

In practice: 𝑅𝑃𝑡 is often positive when 𝑓𝑡,𝑡+1 is negative

• Think of JPY/AUD. Domestic interest rates (in AUD) are higher than foreign interest rates (in JPY). So 𝑓𝑡,𝑡+1 < 0

• AUD depreciates in bad times against JPY, so 𝑅𝑃𝑡 > 0

 31

Summary

• Well over one hundred papers document, in some form or another, the forward premium anomaly - namely, that future exchange rate changes do not move one-for-one with interest rate differentials across countries.

• In 1 to 6 months horizons: consistent evidence of the forward premium anomaly over many currencies.

• The average value of b is -0.88 (Froot & Thaler, 1990).

• Magnitude of b is time varying (possibly regime specific).

• Over the long run though, the average interest rate differentials and currency depreciation move as expected by the theory.

• Possible explanation for the anomaly: risk premium

 32

Two distinct forecasting techniques

• Fundamental analysis

• Econometric/statistical models

• Economic and financial fundamentals (Lecture 4)

• Technical analysis

• Chartists (e.g., “head and shoulders” pattern)

• Statistical analysis (filters / regressions / momentum)

 34

Fundamental analysis

Last Week:

• Balance of Payment

• Economic Growth

• Commodity Prices

• Interest Rates

• Economic Time

iLab assignment?

 35

Technical analysis

Idea that currencies follow trends and reversals

• Majority of strategies based on the temporal evolution of a currency

• Filter-based strategies ("trading rules")

• Purpose: to distinguish trends and reversals from "noise".

• See Menkhoff and Taylor (2007) for a review of the literature on these strategies and their use in practice.

 36

Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2009), p. 360  37

Technical analysis

Negative view of economists

• Reason: inconsistent with the efficient market hypothesis.

Used by the majority of exchange rate traders

• Mainly short term

• Examples: surveys by Taylor and Allen (1992), Lui and Mole (1998), Menkhoff (1998)

• In the long term, fundamental analysis is favoured

• Why? Low frequency fundamental data only

 38

Technical analysis in practice

 39 Source: Menkhoff and Taylor (2007), p. 941.

Analysis by Dick and Menkhoff (2013):

• Directional forecasts of 400 professional traders

• Monthly data: USD/EUR, 1999-2011

Decisions

• « Technical » traders follow trends more and change direction faster than « fundamentalists ».

• Traders become more « fundamentalist » when a currency deviates too much from its long term value (PPP).

Performance

• « Technical » traders perform better in the short term

• Fundamentalists perform better in the long run

• Similar on average  40

• Strategy for short or long term cycles

• Profitable strategy on different currencies

• Examples: Levich and Thomas (1993), LeBaron (1999)

Yet…. is profitability due to luck?

 41

1. Generate artificial exchange rate data

• Predictable only with the forward premium

• Random innovations => no trend

• 10,000 different series

2. Apply the strategy to each series

3. Measuring performance => empirical distribution of profits

4. Test the null hypothesis: performance on currencies = performance on artificial data

 42

Results from Levich and Thomas (1993):

• Strong evidence against the null hypothesis

• Currency profitability better than the top 1% of the simulated series

Recently:

• Less profitable on these same currencies but profitability remains similar on new currencies (emerging countries)

• Pukthuanthong-Le, Levich and Thomas (2007)

 43

Technical analysis

Is it really profitable?

Yes, even taking into account

• « Data mining/snooping »

• Given enough computer time, we are sure that we can find a mechanical trading rule which 'works' on a table of random numbers, Levy (1967)

• Transaction costs

See Hsu, Taylor and Wang (2016) for more details

 44

Momentum

Strategy based on momentum between currencies

Buying currencies that have appreciated in the past

+

Selling currencies that have depreciated in the past

=

Positive (excess) return

See Menkhoff, Sarno, Schmeling, and Schrimpf (2012):

• Cross-section analysis of 48 currencies (1976-2010)  45

Momentum

Strategy implemented:

Every month

1. Rank currencies based on past performance (relative to USD):

period f from 1 to 12 months

2. Create 6 currency portfolios

3. Buying the most winning currencies (portfolio 6) & selling the most losing currencies (portfolio 1)

4. Rebalance after h months

Source: Menkhoff et al (2012).  46

Momentum

Returns from this strategy:

• depend on the number of currencies analyzed

• independent of carry trade and moving averages

• Reduced by transaction costs

• Concentrated purchase in risky currencies with high bid-ask spreads

• vary over time

Source: Menkhoff et al (2012).  47

Role of commodity prices for forecasting exchange rates

Remember (from last lecture) that currencies of commodity-exporting countries tend to appreciate with higher commodity prices.

Source: Jeanneret and Sokolovski (2024, Working Paper)  k ∆ si , t + k

FORECASTING EXCHANGE RATES ∆ si , t + k = si , t + k − si , t t t+ k

Role of commodity prices for forecasting exchange rates

Exchange rate predictability with commodity prices:

d

Source: Jeanneret and Sokolovski (2024, Working Paper) 

Exchange rate predictability depends on trading activity:

Source: Jeanneret and Sokolovski (2024, Working Paper)  50   / Introduction to international finance /
